title: Emergency Guide: Dog Having a Seizure Lasting More Than 5 Minutes
meta_desc: A seizure lasting over 5 minutes is a critical emergency. Learn what status epilepticus is and why your dog needs immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: A seizure lasting more than five minutes is a life-threatening medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent permanent brain damage or organ failure. Transport your pet to the nearest emergency animal hospital right away if they are experiencing status epilepticus.

What is a prolonged seizure and how does it affect my pet?

A seizure is a sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain. While a brief seizure is frightening, a seizure that lasts more than five minutes is a specific medical condition known as status epilepticus. This occurs when the brain is unable to stop the seizure activity on its own, leading to a continuous state of neurological distress.

Is a seizure lasting more than five minutes a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for a seizure lasting more than five minutes is High/Critical and constitutes a life-threatening emergency.
  • Prolonged seizures can lead to a dangerous rise in body temperature (hyperthermia), permanent brain damage, and organ failure.
  • If your dog is currently in this state, transport them to the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately.

How do videos or photos of the seizure help the veterinary team with triage?

  • Capture a short video of the seizure activity only if it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the emergency clinic.
  • Allow veterinary professionals to observe specific muscle movements, facial twitching, and consciousness levels to differentiate between types of seizures or neurological events.
  • Provide visual evidence that helps the vet determine the best course of immediate treatment upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For a dog experiencing a seizure lasting more than 5 minutes (status epilepticus), emergency treatment may include a constant rate infusion of propofol at 0.1-0.6 mg/kg/min. Diazepam can be administered at 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr as a constant-rate infusion to control persistent status epilepticus. If phenobarbital is contraindicated due to a pre-existing hepatic condition, levetiracetam can be given at 40-60 mg/kg IV, SC, or rectally. Phenytoin at 2-5 mg/kg as a slow IV infusion has also been used to stop a seizure. In some cases, inhalation anesthesia with constant monitoring and mechanical ventilation may be necessary.

Chapter: Neurology, Emergency, Pharmacology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1220)

faqs.question: What should I do during the seizure?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog away from stairs or sharp furniture, do not put your hands near their mouth as they may bite transitionally, and keep the environment quiet and dark while you prepare for transport.

faqs.question: Will the seizure stop on its own after 5 minutes?
faqs.answer: Once a seizure passes the five-minute mark, it is unlikely to stop without medical intervention. Emergency medication is usually required to break the cycle.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human seizure medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications to your dog. Only a veterinarian can safely provide the appropriate dosage of anticonvulsants required to stop a status epilepticus event.
